item: App Icon + Splash + Launch Screen Design
definition_origin: App icons are the brand's first contact point on home screens, search results, and notifications; splash/launch screens occupy the first 200-2000ms after tap, masking native cold-start latency. Icons originated as 32x32 desktop pixel art (Susan Kare for Apple, 1984), evolved through skeuomorphic detail (iOS 6), flat design (iOS 7, Material 1), and now Apple's iOS 26 Liquid Glass (translucent multi-layer with system-driven specular lighting) and Android adaptive icons (foreground+background composited under any mask shape). Launch screens replaced static splash images on Android 12+ via the SplashScreen API, which uses your launcher icon plus a brand color rather than a custom image.
core_purpose: Visceral level (Norman): the icon and splash are the brand's first emotional impression — a 50-100ms judgment of trustworthiness, polish, and personality before any text is read. The icon must survive at 40x40px on a cluttered home screen and still feel alive. Behavioral: launch screens hide cold-start latency (the perceived speed gain of an empty-but-branded screen vs. a black screen is ~600-900ms even though actual boot time is identical). Reflective: returning to the icon weeks later, the user wants the small dopamine of recognition — Duolingo's owl, Notion's paper-fold N, Headspace's orange dot — that says 'this is the app where I [feel a thing]'. For an archetype-driven brand, the icon is the single most-seen brand artifact, and is the first place archetype must show — Magician/Creator wants depth and unexpected craft (Linear's gradient mark, Arc's chromatic A); Caregiver wants warmth and softness (Headspace orange dot, Calm's gradient pebble); Jester/Outlaw wants rule-breaking (Duolingo's manic owl, Cash App's green sharp wedge).
when_needed: Mandatory for any consumer mobile or B2B mobile app — App Store and Play Store both reject submissions without a 1024x1024 icon. Required for PWAs (manifest icons 192/512 + maskable). Splash screen mandatory on iOS (storyboard-based) and Android 12+ (SplashScreen API, automatic from launcher icon if not customized). Marketing landing pages need a 'web favicon' equivalent (32x32 ICO + 180x180 apple-touch-icon + 512x512 maskable PNG). AI-wrapper apps especially need this — users judge AI tools by polish first because the underlying model is opaque.
format_options: Master file: 1024x1024 PNG (RGB, no alpha for App Store) is the industry source-of-truth. Apple iOS 26 introduces .icon bundles via Icon Composer — multi-layer artwork with depth groups, specular settings, exported as a single bundle that renders dynamically across Default/Dark/Clear/Tinted/Mono variants. SVG is the working source format for vector icons (Figma, Illustrator, Sketch); never ship SVG as the production icon — it's rasterized to PNG. Android adaptive icon: two PNGs at 432x432 (108dp @ xxxhdpi), foreground may have alpha, background must be opaque, plus optional monochrome layer for Material You themed icons. Splash: iOS uses .storyboard (LaunchScreen.storyboard) referencing PNG/PDF assets; Expo abstracts this via expo-splash-screen plugin. Android 12+ takes a 1024x1024 icon (foreground only) which the system masks into a circle and animates. Lottie (.json) is supported for animated splash on Android via expo-splash-screen v0.27+ but adds 50-200KB. Pros/cons: PNG (universal, but baked size-per-density); SVG (resolution-independent but no system effects); .icon bundle (best iOS 26 fidelity, requires Icon Composer + Xcode 26+); Lottie splash (delightful, payload cost). Target: iOS 1024x1024 PNG opaque (under 1MB); Android adaptive 432x432x2 PNGs (under 100KB each); web favicon ICO under 50KB; maskable PNG 512x512 under 100KB.
file_size_performance: Master 1024 PNG: keep under 1MB (App Store hard limit), realistically 200-500KB for typical icons after pngquant/oxipng compression. Adaptive icon foreground+background combined: under 200KB. Web favicon: 32x32 ICO under 5KB; apple-touch-icon 180x180 PNG under 50KB. Splash Lottie: target under 100KB JSON, drop to 50KB if possible. Android 12+ system splash uses your launcher icon (no extra payload). Use ImageOptim (Mac), TinyPNG, or oxipng (CLI, lossless) before submission.
free_tools: 1. **Icon Composer (Apple, FREE with Xcode 26)** — Mac app, official tool for iOS 26 Liquid Glass multi-layer icons with specular highlights, light/dark/tinted variants, and depth groups. Download via Xcode > Open Developer Tool > Icon Composer or from Apple's Additional Tools page. Required for any app targeting iOS 26 with native Liquid Glass appearance. 2. **Figma Free** — 3 collaborative files free; the Apple Design Resources iOS 26 + Android Material 3 templates are free downloads from Apple Developer and Google. Figma's free plan handles single-designer icon work indefinitely. 3. **Bakery (icon-only Mac app, FREE tier)** — generates iOS/Android/web sizes from one SVG/PNG; free tier covers personal use. 4. **EAS Asset Generator + expo-splash-screen** (FREE for Expo users) — give it a 1024 master + bg color, it outputs every density. 5. **Recraft (FREE 50 credits/day, regenerates daily)** — the only AI tool that natively outputs editable SVG; useful for generating icon concepts you then refine in Figma. 6. **Microsoft Designer (FREE 15 boosts/day)** — DALL-E 3 powered, decent for early icon concept exploration. 7. **Google ImageFX (FREE during research preview, ~50-100 images/day dynamic limit)** — Imagen 3-powered, excellent for stylized illustrative icons. 8. **MakeAppIcon, Icon Kitchen** — free web tools that take 1024x1024 and emit every iOS/Android density + adaptive layers; Icon Kitchen specifically supports Android 12+ themed monochrome layer.
paid_tools: **Starter tier ($10-15/mo)**: Figma Professional ($15/mo) only if collaborating with someone else — solo dev should stay free. Recraft Basic ($12/mo, 1500 credits, full SVG export, no watermark) is the highest-leverage paid upgrade for a non-designer because it produces editable vectors. **Pro tier ($20-30/mo)**: Adobe Firefly ($22.99/mo if not bundled) — only if you need IP-indemnified output for a B2B contract; otherwise skip. Sketch ($10/mo) if you must use Apple-native templates not yet ported to Figma. **Enterprise ($50+)**: Adobe Creative Cloud full ($59.99/mo) — overkill for icon work; only justified if you also do video/print. **Verdict for solo broke dev**: stay on Icon Composer + Figma Free + Recraft Free + Gemini Pro (already paid). Do not upgrade until shipping >2 apps simultaneously.
gemini_pro_workflow:
Concrete steps using Gemini Pro for an app icon:

**Step 1 — Concept exploration with Gemini 2.5 Pro chat**: Brief the model with archetype + brand attributes. Prompt: 'I'm designing an app icon for [app name], a [category] app. Brand archetype is [Magician/Creator]. The icon must work at 40x40 on a home screen, feel emotionally [warm/edgy/precise], and avoid AI-slop tells like gradient mesh blobs, generic 3D rendered orbs, or chibi mascots. Generate 10 textual concept descriptions, each one sentence, each radically different in metaphor.' Pick 3 favorites.

**Step 2 — Imagen 4 in Gemini app**: Open Gemini app, switch to Imagen 4 image generation. Prompt template: 'iOS app icon, [concept], flat vector illustration, single dominant shape, [2-3 specific colors with hex], centered composition with breathing room, 1024x1024, no text, no gradient mesh, no glassmorphism, sharp clean edges, design-system quality.' Generate 8-12 candidates per concept.

**Step 3 — Whisk (until 2026-04-30 sunset; afterward Flow)**: Use a reference image of a brand you admire (e.g., Linear, Arc) as Style input + your own rough sketch as Subject. Whisk blends them. Note: Whisk shuts down April 30, 2026 — features migrate to Flow at labs.google/fx/tools/flow.

**Step 4 — Refine in Recraft (free tier) for SVG output**: Paste the best Imagen output, prompt 'redraw as flat vector SVG, simplified shapes, no gradient' — gives editable paths.

**Step 5 — Gem (custom Gemini)**: Build an 'Icon Critic' Gem with system prompt: 'You are a senior brand designer. Critique this icon for: home-screen legibility at 40px, archetype fit, AI-slop tells, balance, color choice. Be ruthless.' Run final candidates through it.

**Step 6 — NotebookLM**: Drop in Apple HIG (Liquid Glass section) + Material 3 adaptive icon docs + your brand brief; ask 'does my icon comply with all submission requirements?' for a final pre-flight check.
workflow_steps: 1. **Brief (30 min)**: Write one sentence — 'archetype, dominant emotion, 3 things the icon must NOT look like.' Lock target archetype and 2-3 brand colors. 2. **Reference board (45 min)**: Mobbin or Apple's New Design Gallery 2026 — collect 20 icons you envy, label each with 'why it works.' 3. **Concept generation (60 min)**: Gemini Imagen 4, 30-50 candidates across 3-4 metaphor directions; do not iterate on one yet. 4. **Cull (15 min)**: Print candidates as a tiny grid. Anything that doesn't survive at thumbnail is dead. Pick top 3. 5. **Vectorize (90 min)**: Open Figma, manually redraw each top candidate as clean vector — straight lines, even curves, on 1024x1024 with 88px safe margin. (Recraft can give a head-start but always finish in Figma.) 6. **Variants (60 min)**: Build Default/Dark/Tinted manually for iOS 26; build foreground+background+monochrome for Android. 7. **Icon Composer (60 min, iOS 26 only)**: Import layers, set depth groups, adjust specular highlights, preview Liquid Glass. 8. **Splash setup (30 min)**: For Expo, edit app.json's expo-splash-screen plugin — solid color background + the icon centered (image: ./assets/splash-icon.png, imageWidth: 200, backgroundColor: '#hex'). 9. **Device test (45 min)**: TestFlight build + APK side-load. Check home screen, settings, notifications, share sheet. 10. **Compress + ship (15 min)**: Run all PNGs through pngquant or ImageOptim; commit to repo. **Total**: ~7-8 hours for a first icon, ~3 hours for revisions.
ai_prompt_templates:
**Template 1 — Iconic mark (Magician/Creator archetype)**: 'iOS app icon, single bold geometric symbol representing {concept_metaphor}, flat vector design, two-color palette {primary_hex} and {accent_hex} on {background_hex}, centered composition, 88px breathing room from edges, sharp clean vector edges, design-system precision, no gradient mesh, no glassmorphism, no 3D render, no text, no isometric perspective, 1024x1024 square, professional brand mark.'

**Template 2 — Mascot face (Caregiver/Jester archetype)**: 'iOS app icon, friendly {animal_or_creature} face fills the frame from chin to top of head, large expressive eyes with subtle highlight, soft rounded shapes, two-tone fill plus one accent color {color_palette_hex}, flat illustration with subtle hand-drawn wobble in linework, centered, no body visible, no AI-generated melted features, no six-fingered hands, 1024x1024.'

**Template 3 — Abstract gradient (caution: high slop risk)**: 'iOS app icon, two-stop linear gradient from {color_a_hex} to {color_b_hex} forming a single hard-edged geometric shape — circle/squircle/wedge — on solid {bg_hex} background, single soft drop-shadow at 30% opacity, no mesh gradient, no chromatic aberration, no glassy reflection, 1024x1024.'

**Template 4 — Letterform (B2B/dev tool)**: 'App icon, single letter "{X}" rendered in custom geometric sans-serif, weight ExtraBold, occupying 60% of canvas height, centered, color {fg_hex} on {bg_hex}, optical adjustments for the {X} terminals, 88px safe margin, no swooshes, no metallic effect, 1024x1024 PNG opaque.'
claude_code_integration: For an Expo/React Native repo, drop the master files at `apps/mobile/assets/images/icon.png` (1024x1024) and `apps/mobile/assets/images/splash-icon.png` (1024x1024). Update `app.json` with the `expo-splash-screen` plugin block (see Expo docs) — Claude Code can write this config from a one-line spec. EAS Build auto-generates all densities at build time; no manual size set required. For iOS 26 Liquid Glass: store the .icon bundle at `apps/mobile/ios/{AppName}/Assets.xcassets/AppIcon.appiconset/` and reference via `ios.icon` field in app.json (Expo SDK 51+). Install runtime libs: none required for icons; for splash use `npx expo install expo-splash-screen` and call `SplashScreen.preventAutoHideAsync()` before your auth bootstrap, then `SplashScreen.hideAsync()` once your initial route is ready. Optimization script: add a `scripts/optimize-icons.sh` running `oxipng -o4 assets/images/*.png` before commits. For web/marketing: drop favicons in `apps/web/public/`, add `<link rel='apple-touch-icon' sizes='180x180' href='/apple-touch-icon.png'>` and `<link rel='manifest' href='/manifest.json'>` referencing maskable 512x512.
emotional_design_integration: **Visceral**: archetype shows in shape language — Magician = depth + unexpected detail (Arc's chromatic A morphs hue based on time); Caregiver = soft squircle + warm pastel (Calm's pebble); Jester = asymmetry + raised eyebrow (Duolingo's manic owl pose); Hero = bold geometric clarity (Strava's chevron); Outlaw = sharp angles + high contrast (Cash App's green wedge). **Behavioral**: legibility at 40px is non-negotiable — every Magician/Creator app fails here when it overloads detail; the discipline is to remove until the silhouette alone reads. **Reflective**: variants are where archetype deepens — Apple's iOS 26 Tinted mode means your icon reduces to a monochrome silhouette; if your archetype only works in full color, you're brittle. The Caregiver should feel warm in Tinted (organic curves); the Magician should still feel uncanny in Tinted (unusual silhouette). For our user's Magician/Creator + Jester/Outlaw + Caregiver mix: design the silhouette first as if for Tinted mode, then color it for Default — this forces shape-first thinking.
specific_examples: 1. **Linear** (https://linear.app) — Magician/Creator. Single chromatic gradient diamond on near-black, microscopic precision in vector geometry. Why it works: silhouette reads at 40px AND the gradient feels like 'precision lighting' not 'AI mesh' because edges are razor-hard. 2. **Arc by The Browser Company** (https://arc.net) — Creator/Magician. The icon literally cycles colors based on time of day; the multi-layer Apple Icon Composer treatment with parallax is one of the best Liquid Glass adopters. 3. **Duolingo** (https://duolingo.com) — Jester/Caregiver hybrid. Cylindrical owl with a wing, simplified to a few primitives so it can animate at low cost; the 'manic green owl' personality is fully visible at 40px. 4. **Headspace** — Caregiver. A single orange dot on white. Won't win design awards but the simplicity = nervous-system relief, perfectly on-archetype. 5. **Cash App** — Outlaw. Sharp green wedge S, no friendliness — the brand says 'banking but rebellious' visually. 6. **Things 3 by Cultured Code** — Magician/Sage. Hand-drawn-feeling check mark on warm yellow; the slight imperfection is the design choice. 7. **Notion** — Sage/Creator. The N that folds like paper — referenced metaphor + minimal mark. 8. **Apple's New Design Gallery 2026** (https://developer.apple.com/design/new-design-gallery-2026/) — Apple's curated showcase of icons that nail Liquid Glass.
anti_ai_slop_traps: **Tells specific to AI-generated icons**: (a) Mesh gradient soup — purple-to-cyan blobs with no hard edges; if your icon could be a Mac OS Big Sur wallpaper, it's slop. (b) Generic 3D blobby orb — Imagen and Midjourney love rendering 'glossy 3D pill on neutral background'; this is the #1 give-away. (c) Glassmorphic stacked rectangles — three translucent rectangles with rainbow borders is now a slop signature. (d) Six-fingered hands or melted faces if your concept includes a character. (e) Symmetrical-but-wrong — AI symmetry is 'too clean' (perfect mirror with subtle pixel-level chaos); human-designed symmetry has intentional micro-asymmetries. (f) Too many micro-details — AI cannot resist adding ornament; if your icon at 40px becomes mush, it's over-detailed. (g) Default Midjourney lighting — top-left key light + soft shadow at 4 o'clock, every time. (h) Pastel mauve + minty teal palette (the 'Stripe gradient' became the 'AI default'). **Counter-prompts**: 'no gradient mesh', 'no 3D render', 'no glassmorphism', 'flat vector only', 'two flat colors maximum', 'sharp pixel-perfect edges', 'no chromatic aberration'. **Counter-process**: always finish in Figma by manually redrawing — never ship a raw AI raster. Print at 1cm on paper; if you can't read it from arm's length, kill it.
common_pitfalls: Submitting RGBA (alpha) to App Store — auto-rejected; always export with opaque background. Forgetting Android adaptive layers — uploading only a square PNG means Android masks badly on Pixel/Samsung launchers. Skipping the iOS 26 Tinted variant — your icon will look broken in Tinted mode if not designed for monochrome. Using transparency in the foreground when the safe-zone gets clipped (Android masks aggressively, design within the inner 66dp circle). Ignoring the Android 12 splash — if you don't customize, the system uses your launcher icon on a white background; for dark-themed apps this looks broken. Splash image too large — Android 12 SplashScreen API will clip if your icon image exceeds 108dp. Putting text in the icon — App Store reviewers reject icons that are just the brand name in a font. Using a different icon for App Store than for the home screen — Apple requires they match. Not testing on a real device — simulator masks differ from real launchers. Forgetting the web favicon set (16/32/180/512 maskable) — hurts PWA install and link previews.
accessibility_notes: Icons are decorative, not functional, so no alt text needed in iOS Springboard. For web favicons, the page title carries the meaning. Color contrast: ensure your icon hits 3:1 contrast against both light and dark wallpapers (test against pure white AND pure black backgrounds). Reduced-motion: if you use animated splash (Lottie or Android 12 AnimatedVectorDrawable), provide a static fallback when `UIAccessibility.isReduceMotionEnabled` is true on iOS or when `Settings.Global.ANIMATOR_DURATION_SCALE` is 0 on Android. Color-blind safety: test your icon under deuteranopia and tritanopia simulation (Stark plugin in Figma). Avoid red-green as the only differentiator. For tinted/monochrome variants on iOS 26 and Android themed icons, ensure the silhouette alone communicates the brand — no information should be color-only.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile: native runtime composites the icon under platform-specific masks (squircle on iOS, circle/squircle/teardrop on Android depending on OEM launcher); requires multi-layer source for Android adaptive + iOS 26 Liquid Glass. Web: the favicon stack (.ico 16/32, apple-touch-icon 180, manifest 192/512, maskable 512) is flatter; no system specular effects, but PWA install promotes a maskable icon to home screen on Android Chrome — design with safe zone. Marketing OG image is a separate asset (1200x630). Splash is mobile-only — web uses a skeleton UI instead.
